description: distractor batch 10 (final) - options strategies, swaps/forwards/futures, currency management, principles of AA, AA real-world constraints, AA to alternatives, CME (178 items); parallel-weight distractors authored; correct key pinned to post-rotation layout; closes the 1,183-item distractor queue
precondition: apply after batch_01, batch_02, rotate_drill_keys.py, batch_03, batch_04, batch_05, batch_06, batch_07, batch_08, batch_09

options.A: A synthetic long put is created by combining long stock with a short call on the same underlying.
options.B: short stock (or short forward/futures) + long call — the symmetric payoff of a short stock position becomes asymmetrical if transformed into a synthetic long put by buying a call.
options.C: A synthetic long put is created by combining long stock with a long call on the same underlying.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Long stock plus short call is a COVERED CALL — its payoff resembles a short put, not a long put.
rationales.B:
Full statement: short stock (or short forward/futures) + long call — the symmetric payoff of a short stock position becomes asymmetrical if transformed into a synthetic long put by buying a call; payoffs are identical to a long put at the same strike.

Correct. Per Synthetic Put and Call: 'the symmetric payoff of a short stock position can become asymmetrical if the investor transforms it into a synthetic long put position by buying a call... payoffs from this synthetic put position at various stock prices at option expiration are identical to those of a long put with a 50-strike price.'
rationales.C: Incorrect. Long stock plus long call doubles the bullish exposure; the synthetic long put pairs a SHORT stock (or forward) position with a long call.

options.A: The only motivation for a synthetic long forward is avoiding margin requirements on the actual forward.
options.B: Synthetic forward positions are created solely to increase transaction costs for tax purposes.
options.C: to exploit an ARBITRAGE opportunity presented by the actual forward price OR the need for an ALTERNATIVE to the outright purchase of a long forward position.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Margin treatment is incidental; the motivations are ARBITRAGE against the actual forward price or an alternative route to the exposure.
rationales.B: Incorrect. Cost inflation is never the goal — mispricing capture or an outright-purchase alternative drives the construction.
rationales.C:
Full statement: to exploit an ARBITRAGE opportunity presented by the actual forward price OR the need for an ALTERNATIVE to the outright purchase of a long forward position; frequently a forward contract is used instead of futures to acquire a stock position because it allows for contract customization.

Correct. Per Position Equivalencies: 'The motivation to create a synthetic long forward position could be to exploit an arbitrage opportunity presented by the actual forward price or the need for an alternative to the outright purchase of a long forward position. Frequently, a forward contract is used instead of futures to acquire a stock position because it allows for contract customization.'

options.A: the market maker exercises her call, pays the strike × shares, receives the shares, then delivers to client and receives the forward price × shares.
options.B: The market maker hedging a short forward simply waits until expiration and buys shares in the open market at the prevailing price.
options.C: The market maker's synthetic hedge leaves her exposed to unlimited losses if the stock rallies before delivery.

rationales.A:
Full statement: the market maker exercises her call, pays the strike × shares, receives the shares, then delivers to client and receives the forward price × shares; if BELOW strike, the long put owner exercises, the market maker receives the shares for the strike, then delivers to the client and receives the forward price — either way the position is fully hedged.

Correct. Per Example 1: 'At the expiry of the forward contract, if the stock price is above 200.35p, the market maker exercises her call, pays £20,035, and receives 10,000 Vodafone shares. She then delivers these shares to the client and receives £20,035. At the expiry of the forward contract, if the stock price is below 200.35p, the owner of the long put will exercise his option, and the market maker receives the 10,000 Vodafone shares for £20,035. She then delivers these shares to the client and receives £20,035.'
rationales.B: Incorrect. Open-market purchase at expiry leaves the price risk unhedged throughout — the synthetic (long call + short put at the forward strike) locks the price now.
rationales.C: Incorrect. The synthetic long forward neutralizes the rally scenario: she exercises the call, pays the strike, receives shares, and delivers to the client.

options.A: (X − S₀) + c₀ = 30.00 − 25.00 + 1.55 = 6.55 — the covered call writer participates in gains up to the exercise price, after which further appreciation is lost to the call buyer.

rationales.A:
Full statement: (X − S₀) + c₀ = 30.00 − 25.00 + 1.55 = 6.55 — the covered call writer participates in gains up to the exercise price, after which further appreciation is lost to the call buyer; X − S₀ = 5.00 in appreciation to the strike, plus 1.55 premium.

Correct. Per Example 3: 'C is correct. The covered call writer participates in gains up to the exercise price, after which further appreciation is lost to the call buyer. That is, X − S₀ = 30.00 − 25.00 = 5.00. The call writer also keeps c₀, the option premium, which is 1.55. So, the total maximum gain is 5.00 + 1.55 = 6.55.'
rationales.B: Incorrect. This uses the terminal price as the gain endpoint; the covered call's upside stops at the STRIKE — (X − S₀) + c₀.
rationales.C: Incorrect. This nets the premium in the wrong direction and ignores the call-away at 30; the writer keeps c₀ = 1.55 and gains only up to the strike.

options.A: The cost of put protection is reduced by choosing a HIGHER strike price closer to the current stock price.
options.B: The deductible in a protective put is fixed by the exchange and cannot be adjusted by the portfolio manager.
options.C: the portfolio manager can REDUCE the cost of insurance by INCREASING the size of the deductible (current stock price minus put exercise price) — for example, using a lower exercise price.

rationales.A: Incorrect. A higher strike SHRINKS the deductible and RAISES the premium — the opposite of the cost-reduction move.
rationales.B: Incorrect. The deductible (current price minus put strike) is entirely the manager's choice through strike selection — a lower strike means a larger deductible and cheaper insurance.
rationales.C:
Full statement: the portfolio manager can REDUCE the cost of insurance by INCREASING the size of the deductible (current stock price minus put exercise price) — for example, using a lower exercise price; a lower-strike put has a lower premium but does not protect against losses until the stock falls to that lower strike.

Correct. Per Loss Protection/Upside Preservation: 'The portfolio manager can also reduce the cost of insurance by increasing the size of the deductible (i.e., the current stock price minus the put exercise price), perhaps by using a put option with a 14 exercise price. A put option with an exercise price of 14 would have a lower premium but would not protect against losses in the stock until it falls to 14.00 per share.'

options.A: After an adverse event materializes, the protective put holder should always hold the put to expiration to collect remaining time value.
options.B: Once purchased, a protective put cannot be sold before its expiration date.
options.C: a price shock to the underlying may INCREASE the market's expectations of future volatility, thereby likely increasing the put premium.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Holding surrenders the volatility premium — the shock typically RAISES implied volatility and the put's price, favoring an early sale.
rationales.B: Incorrect. Exchange-listed puts trade continuously; selling early after the event captures both intrinsic gain and the inflated volatility component.
rationales.C:
Full statement: a price shock to the underlying may INCREASE the market's expectations of future volatility, thereby likely increasing the put premium; by selling the option early, the investor would capture this increase; once the adverse event has occurred there may be no reason to continue holding the insurance — sell the put and recapture some of its cost.

Correct. Per Investment Objectives of Protective Puts footnote: 'A price shock to the underlying asset might increase the market's expectations of future volatility, thereby likely increasing the put premium. By selling the option early, the investor would capture this increase. Also, once the adverse event occurred, there may be no reason to continue to hold the insurance. If the investor no longer needs it, he should cancel it and get part of the purchase price back. In other words, he should sell the put and recapture some of its cost.'

options.A: such continuous purchasing of puts to protect against a possible stock price decline is an EXPENSIVE strategy that would wipe out most of the long-term gain on an otherwise good investment.
options.B: Continuously purchasing protective puts over the long term is always advisable for any equity portfolio.
options.C: The cost of a rolling protective put program is negligible relative to portfolio returns.

rationales.A:
Full statement: such continuous purchasing of puts to protect against a possible stock price decline is an EXPENSIVE strategy that would wipe out most of the long-term gain on an otherwise good investment; the occasional purchase of a protective put to manage a temporary situation, however, can be a sensible risk-reducing activity.

Correct. Per Protective Puts: 'Such continuous purchasing of puts to protect against a possible stock price decline is an expensive strategy that would wipe out most of the long-term gain on an otherwise good investment. The occasional purchase of a protective put to manage a temporary situation, however, can be a sensible risk-reducing activity.'
rationales.B: Incorrect. The strategy is EXPENSIVE — repeated premium outlays can consume the equity risk premium the portfolio exists to earn.
rationales.C: Incorrect. Rolling premiums compound into a material drag; the reading flags long-term continuous protection as wealth-eroding.

options.A: The position delta of long 100 shares plus one long ATM put is approximately +100.
options.B: The position delta of long 100 shares plus one long ATM put is approximately 0.
options.C: +50 — long stock delta +100 plus ATM long put delta approximately −0.5 × 100 = −50; overall +100 − 50 = +50.

rationales.A: Incorrect. +100 ignores the put entirely; the ATM put's ≈ −0.5 × 100 = −50 offsets half the stock delta.
rationales.B: Incorrect. Zero would require a deep-ITM put with delta near −1; the ATM put only halves the exposure to +50.
rationales.C: Correct. Per Delta section: 'Compare this call with a protective put, in which someone buys 100 shares of stock and one contract of an ATM put. Its position delta would also be 50: +100 points for the stock and −50 points for the long put.'

options.A: as the stock drops in value, the investor profits from the short stock position but portfolio gains will be REDUCED by the short put.
options.B: Selling a put against a short stock position increases the portfolio's profit as the stock falls without limit.
options.C: Selling a put against a short stock position provides complete protection against a rally in the stock.

rationales.A:
Full statement: as the stock drops in value, the investor profits from the short stock position but portfolio gains will be REDUCED by the short put; when share price increases, short position loses money, the put expires worthless and the investor keeps the put premium — the loss on the short can still be substantial but is somewhat reduced by the put option premium.

Correct. Per Buying Calls and Writing Puts on a Short Position: 'To generate income from option premiums, the investor may also sell a put. As the stock drops in value, the investor profits from the short stock position, but the portfolio (− Put − Stock) gains will be reduced by the short put. When the share price increases, the short position loses money. The put expires worthless, meaning the investor will keep the put premium. The loss on the short position can still be substantial but is somewhat reduced by the put option premium.'
rationales.B: Incorrect. The short put CAPS the downside gain — below the strike, put losses offset short-stock profits.
rationales.C: Incorrect. The premium is only a small cushion against a rally; upside losses on the short stock continue beyond it.

options.A: Both structures produce identical portfolio deltas, since a long call and a short put carry the same delta.
options.B: long call + short stock: portfolio delta = 0.516 − 1 = −0.484 (bearish, less bearish than short alone due to call).
options.C: The short stock plus long call combination has a positive portfolio delta, making it a bullish position.

rationales.A: Incorrect. The deltas differ: 0.516 − 1 = −0.484 for the call variant versus −0.335 − 1 = −1.335 for the short-put variant.
rationales.B:
Full statement: long call + short stock: portfolio delta = 0.516 − 1 = −0.484 (bearish, less bearish than short alone due to call); short put + short stock: portfolio delta = − (−0.335) − 1 = −0.665 (still bearish, only slightly less due to put premium); both remain bearish overall.

Correct. Per Buying Calls and Writing Puts on a Short Position: 'The delta from the short PBR shares is −1. The SEP 16 call delta is 0.516. The overall portfolio delta is still negative at −0.484, making this a bearish strategy.' And: 'The delta of the short PBR shares is −1, and the delta of the short put is −(−0.335), so the position delta is −1 + 0.335 = −0.665.'
rationales.C: Incorrect. The combined delta remains NEGATIVE (−0.484) — still bearish, merely less bearish than short stock alone.

options.A: The long call on short stock and the short put on short stock have the same vega and theta signs.
options.B: Neither structure carries any vega or theta exposure, since the stock leg dominates.
options.C: long call + short stock: the investor is LONG VEGA (from purchasing call) and exposed to negative theta (time decay hurts).

rationales.A: Incorrect. The signs FLIP between the structures — long call is long vega/negative theta; short put is short vega/positive theta.
rationales.B: Incorrect. The option legs carry real Greeks; the long call buyer pays time decay for positive volatility exposure.
rationales.C:
Full statement: long call + short stock: the investor is LONG VEGA (from purchasing call) and exposed to negative theta (time decay hurts); short put + short stock: the investor is SHORT VEGA (from writing put) and BENEFITS from time decay (positive theta on short put).

Correct. Per Buying Calls and Writing Puts on a Short Position: 'The investor is also long vega from purchasing the call, 0.018, and the position is exposed to time decay, because theta is −0.018 per day.' And: 'She is also short vega from writing the put, (−0.017), and benefits from time decay, as theta of the short put is +0.015 (= −[−0.015]).'

options.A: A collar is a position combining a short underlying with a long ATM call and a long ATM put.
options.B: a collar is an option position in which the investor is LONG the underlying, BUYS a put with exercise price BELOW current stock price (downside protection), and WRITES a call with exercise price ABOVE current stock price (finances put).
options.C: A collar consists of two calls at different strikes with no position in the underlying.

rationales.A: Incorrect. The collar is built on a LONG underlying — protected below by an OTM put and financed by a written OTM call.
rationales.B:
Full statement: a collar is an option position in which the investor is LONG the underlying, BUYS a put with exercise price BELOW current stock price (downside protection), and WRITES a call with exercise price ABOVE current stock price (finances put); the profit and loss on the equity position is limited by the option positions.

Correct. Per Collars: 'A collar is an option position in which the investor is long shares of stock and then buys a put with an exercise price below the current stock price and writes a call with an exercise price above the current stock price. Collars allow a shareholder to acquire downside protection through a protective put but reduce the cash outlay by writing a covered call. By carefully selecting the options, an investor can often offset most of the put premium paid by the call premium received.'
rationales.C: Incorrect. Two calls at different strikes is a call SPREAD; the collar requires the underlying plus the put/call combination around it.

options.A: V_T = S_T + Max(0, X_1 − S_T) − Max(0, S_T − X_2), where X_2 > X_1 (X_1 = long put strike, X_2 = short call strike).
options.B: Collar value at expiration: V_T = S_T − Max(0, X_1 − S_T) + Max(0, S_T − X_2).
options.C: Collar value at expiration: V_T = Max(S_T, X_2) − Min(S_T, X_1).

rationales.A:
Full statement: V_T = S_T + Max(0, X_1 − S_T) − Max(0, S_T − X_2), where X_2 > X_1 (X_1 = long put strike, X_2 = short call strike); profit Π = S_T + Max(0, X_1 − S_T) − Max(0, S_T − X_2) − S₀ − p₀ + c₀.

Correct. Per Collars on an Existing Holding: 'The value of the collar at expiration is the sum of the value of the underlying asset, the value of the long put (struck at X1), and the value of the short call (struck at X2): VT = ST + Max(0,X1 − ST) − Max(0,ST − X2), where X2 > X1. The profit is the profit on the underlying share plus the profit on the long put and the short call so that: Π = ST + Max(0,X1 − ST) − Max(0,ST − X2) − S0 − p0 + c0.'
rationales.B: Incorrect. The signs are reversed — the LONG put value is added and the SHORT call value subtracted.
rationales.C: Incorrect. This max/min construction does not reproduce the collar's payoff; V_T = S_T + Max(0, X_1 − S_T) − Max(0, S_T − X_2).

options.A: As the collar's strikes move farther apart, the position converges toward a riskless fixed-income payoff.
options.B: as chosen put and call exercise prices move successively FARTHER in opposite directions from the current price, the collar begins to replicate the underlying gain/loss pattern of a LONG POSITION in the underlying security.
options.C: The collar's risk-return profile is unaffected by the choice of put and call strikes.

rationales.A: Incorrect. WIDER strikes make the collar MORE equity-like; convergence toward fixed income comes from moving both strikes toward the current price.
rationales.B:
Full statement: as chosen put and call exercise prices move successively FARTHER in opposite directions from the current price, the collar begins to replicate the underlying gain/loss pattern of a LONG POSITION in the underlying security; conversely, as the strikes APPROACH each other, expected returns and volatility become less equity-like and converge on a RISK-FREE FIXED-INCOME return; a collar is economically INTERMEDIATE between pure equity and fixed-income exposure.

Correct. Per Collars on an Existing Holding: 'As the chosen put and call exercise prices move successively farther in opposite directions from the current price, the combined collar position begins to replicate the underlying gain/loss pattern of a long position in the underlying security. Conversely, as the chosen strike prices approach and meet each other, the expected returns and volatility become less and less equity-like and eventually converge on those of a risk-free, fixed income return to the time horizon. Thus, a collar position is, economically, intermediate between pure equity and fixed-income exposure.'
rationales.C: Incorrect. Strike placement is the profile's whole lever — the collar spans the spectrum between pure equity and near-fixed-income.

options.A: For a bear put spread, breakeven is S_T* = X_L + p_H, with maximum profit when the stock rallies above X_H.
options.B: A bear put spread has no defined breakeven point because both legs are puts.
options.C: for a bear put spread with lower strike X_L (short put p_L) and higher strike X_H (long put p_H), breakeven S_T* = X_H − p_H + p_L.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Breakeven sits at X_H minus the NET premium (p_H − p_L), and maximum profit arrives when the stock FALLS to or below X_L.
rationales.B: Incorrect. The breakeven is well-defined: S_T* = X_H − (p_H − p_L), the higher strike less the net cost.
rationales.C:
Full statement: for a bear put spread with lower strike X_L (short put p_L) and higher strike X_H (long put p_H), breakeven S_T* = X_H − p_H + p_L; profit = X_H − S_T − p_H + p_L when X_L < S_T < X_H; max loss (occurs above X_H) = −(p_H − p_L) = p_L − p_H; max gain (occurs at or below X_L) = X_H − X_L − p_H + p_L.

Correct. Per Bear Spread section (V4 LM1 §7): 'The breakeven point, ST* = XH − pH + pL, sets the profit equal to zero between the strike prices. In this example, 16 − 1.96 + 1.46 = 15.50.'

options.A: A debit spread is one in which the investor receives a net cash inflow at initiation.
options.B: The debit/credit distinction refers to whether the options are calls or puts.
options.C: if establishing the spread requires a cash payment by the investor, it is a DEBIT SPREAD (long option value exceeds short option value).

rationales.A: Incorrect. A net INFLOW defines a CREDIT spread; the debit spread requires a net payment because the long leg costs more than the short leg brings in.
rationales.B: Incorrect. Either calls or puts can form debit or credit spreads; the distinction is the direction of the initial cash flow.
rationales.C:
Full statement: if establishing the spread requires a cash payment by the investor, it is a DEBIT SPREAD (long option value exceeds short option value); if the spread initially results in a cash INFLOW, it is a CREDIT SPREAD (short option value exceeds long option value).

Correct. Per Bull Spreads and Bear Spreads: 'If establishing the spread requires a cash payment by the investor, it is referred to as a debit spread. Debit spreads are effectively long because the long option value exceeds the short option value. If the spread initially results in a cash inflow to the investor, it is referred to as a credit spread. Credit spreads are effectively short because the short option value exceeds the long option value.'

options.A: Maximum gain on the OCT 45/50 bull call spread is 1.10.
options.B: Maximum gain on the OCT 45/50 bull call spread is 3.05.

rationales.A: Incorrect. 1.10 is the NET COST (2.55 − 1.45), not the gain.
rationales.B: Incorrect. The maximum gain is the strike width minus the net cost: (50 − 45) − 1.10 = 3.90.
rationales.C: Correct. Per Example 6: 'C is correct. With a bull spread, the maximum gain occurs at the high exercise price. At an underlying price of 50 or higher, the spread is worth the difference in the strike prices, or 50 − 45 = 5. The cost of establishing the spread is the price paid for the lower-strike option minus the price received for the higher-strike option: 2.55 − 1.45 = 1.10. The maximum gain is 5.00 − 1.10 = 3.90.'

options.A: a strategy in which one SELLS an option and BUYS the same type of option (put or call) but with DIFFERENT EXPIRATION DATES on the SAME underlying asset and with the SAME STRIKE.
options.B: A calendar spread combines options of the same type and expiration on two different underlyings.
options.C: A calendar spread combines a call and a put with the same strike and expiration.

rationales.A:
Full statement: a strategy in which one SELLS an option and BUYS the same type of option (put or call) but with DIFFERENT EXPIRATION DATES on the SAME underlying asset and with the SAME STRIKE; when the investor buys the more distant option and sells the near-term option, it is a LONG calendar spread; buying near-term and selling longer-dated is a SHORT calendar spread.

Correct. Per Calendar Spread: 'A strategy in which one sells an option and buys the same type of option but with different expiration dates, on the same underlying asset and with the same strike, is commonly referred to as a calendar spread. When the investor buys the more distant call and sells the near-term call, it is a long calendar spread. The investor could also buy a near-term call and sell a longer-dated one, known as a short calendar spread.'
rationales.B: Incorrect. Same UNDERLYING is required; the legs differ only in expiration date.
rationales.C: Incorrect. Call-plus-put at one strike/expiry is a STRADDLE; the calendar spread is same type, same strike, DIFFERENT expiries.

options.A: a long calendar spread is a LONG VOLATILITY trading strategy because the longer-term option has a HIGHER VEGA than the shorter-term option.
options.B: A long calendar spread is vega-neutral because the two option legs' vegas cancel exactly.
options.C: A long calendar spread is a short volatility strategy that profits when implied volatility falls.

rationales.A:
Full statement: a long calendar spread is a LONG VOLATILITY trading strategy because the longer-term option has a HIGHER VEGA than the shorter-term option; maximum profit is obtained when the short-term option expires worthless, then implied volatility surges, increasing the price of the remaining long-term option.

Correct. Per Investment Objectives and Strategy Selection: 'a long calendar spread—wherein the shorter-maturity option is sold and the longer-maturity option is purchased—is a long volatility trading strategy because the longer-term option has a higher vega than the shorter-term option. The maximum profit is obtained when the short-term option expires worthless, then implied volatility surges, increasing the price of the remaining long-term option.'
rationales.B: Incorrect. The vegas do NOT cancel — the longer-dated leg's vega exceeds the shorter-dated leg's.
rationales.C: Incorrect. The net vega is POSITIVE (long the higher-vega distant option), making the position long volatility.

options.A: Implied volatility is the historical standard deviation of the underlying's returns over the past year.
options.B: Implied volatility is a forecast published by the options exchange each morning.
options.C: implied volatility is derived from an option pricing model (such as BSM) and is the value that EQUATES THE MODEL PRICE OF AN OPTION TO ITS MARKET PRICE.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Historical (realized) volatility looks backward; implied volatility is extracted from CURRENT option prices.
rationales.B: Incorrect. No exchange publishes it as a forecast — it is the volatility input that equates the model (e.g., BSM) price to the observed market price.
rationales.C:
Full statement: implied volatility is derived from an option pricing model (such as BSM) and is the value that EQUATES THE MODEL PRICE OF AN OPTION TO ITS MARKET PRICE; all other BSM inputs (strike, underlying price, time to expiration, risk-free rate) are observable; implied volatilities incorporate investors' expectations about future asset returns and market uncertainty.

Correct. Per Implied Volatility and Volatility Skew: 'Implied volatility is not observable per se, but it is derived from an option pricing model—such as the Black-Scholes-Merton (BSM) model—and it is value that equates the model price of an option to its market price. Note that all other input variables to the BSM model, including the option's strike price, the price of the underlying, the time to option expiration, and the risk-free interest rate, are observable. Implied volatilities incorporate investors' expectations about the future course of financial asset returns and the level of market uncertainty associated with them.'

options.A: A volatility smile is a downward-sloping line, while a skew is any flat volatility curve.
options.B: when implied volatilities of both OTM puts and OTM calls trade at a premium to ATM implied volatilities, the curve is U-shaped — a VOLATILITY SMILE.
options.C: Smile and skew are interchangeable terms for the same implied volatility shape.

rationales.A: Incorrect. The smile is U-SHAPED — both OTM puts and OTM calls trade above ATM implied volatility.
rationales.B:
Full statement: when implied volatilities of both OTM puts and OTM calls trade at a premium to ATM implied volatilities, the curve is U-shaped — a VOLATILITY SMILE; the more common shape is a VOLATILITY SKEW where implied volatility INCREASES for OTM puts and DECREASES for OTM calls as strike moves from current price; skew persists because investors have less interest in OTM calls while OTM puts have universal demand as portfolio insurance.

Correct. Per Implied Volatility and Volatility Skew: 'When the implied volatilities priced into both OTM puts and calls trade at a premium to implied volatilities of ATM options... the curve is U-shaped and is called a volatility smile, because it resembles the shape of a smile. The more common shape of the implied volatility curve, however, is a volatility skew, where the implied volatility increases for OTM puts and decreases for OTM calls, as the strike price moves away from the current price. This shape persists across asset classes and over time because investors have generally less interest in OTM calls whereas OTM put options have found universal demand as portfolio insurance against a market sell-off.'
rationales.C: Incorrect. The shapes differ: the smile is symmetric-U; the skew is ASYMMETRIC, with OTM put IV elevated relative to OTM calls.

options.A: A sharp rise in skew alongside surging implied volatility signals strong bullish sentiment among institutions.
options.B: Skew level changes carry no information about market sentiment.
options.C: when the implied volatility is significantly higher (relative to historical levels) for PUTS with strike prices below the underlying's price, it means an IMBALANCE in the supply and demand for options.

rationales.A: Incorrect. The signal runs BEARISH — investors bidding up downside puts reveals demand for crash protection.
rationales.B: Incorrect. The skew is a sentiment gauge; elevated put IV relative to history flags hedging demand and downside fear.
rationales.C:
Full statement: when the implied volatility is significantly higher (relative to historical levels) for PUTS with strike prices below the underlying's price, it means an IMBALANCE in the supply and demand for options; a sharp increase in skew accompanied by a surge in absolute implied volatility is an INDICATOR THAT MARKET SENTIMENT IS TURNING BEARISH; in contrast, higher IVs for OTM CALLS indicate BULLISH sentiment.

Correct. Per Implied Volatility and Volatility Skew: 'when the implied volatility is significantly higher (relative to historical levels) for puts with strike prices below the underlying asset's price, it means that there is an imbalance in the supply and demand for options... A sharp increase in the level of the skew, accompanied with a surge in the absolute level of implied volatility, is an indicator that market sentiment is turning bearish. In contrast, higher implied volatilities (relative to historical levels) for calls with strike prices above the underlying asset's price indicate that investors are bullish and the demand for OTM calls to take on upside exposure is strong.'

options.A: the 90% moneyness option is a put with strike (X) equal to 90% of the current underlying price (X/S = 90%).
options.B: In the 90-110 skew measure, the 90% moneyness option is a call with strike at 110% of spot.
options.C: The 90-110 skew compares two ATM options with different expiration dates.

rationales.A:
Full statement: the 90% moneyness option is a put with strike (X) equal to 90% of the current underlying price (X/S = 90%); the 110% moneyness of the call is calculated similarly (110%); the volatility skew is calculated as the DIFFERENCE between the IVs of the 90% put and the 110% call; a positive skew number (put IV > call IV) reflects the fat-tail insurance premium demanded for downside protection.

Correct. Per Implied Volatility and Volatility Skew: 'The 90% moneyness option is a put with strike (X) equal to 90% of the current underlying price (S); thus X/S = 90%. The 110% moneyness of the call is calculated similarly. Also shown is the skew, calculated as the difference between the implied volatilities of the 90% put and the 110% call.'
rationales.B: Incorrect. The 90% moneyness leg is a PUT with X/S = 90%; the call leg sits at 110% moneyness.
rationales.C: Incorrect. Expiry is held constant; the measure differences IV across STRIKES — IV(90% put) minus IV(110% call).

options.A: The implied volatility surface is a two-dimensional plot of implied volatility against strike price only.
options.B: a THREE-DIMENSIONAL plot, for put and call options on the same underlying, of days to expiration (x-axis), option strike prices (y-axis), and implied volatilities (z-axis).
options.C: The implied volatility surface plots realized volatility against trading volume.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Strike-only is a single smile/skew slice; the surface adds the EXPIRATION dimension.
rationales.B:
Full statement: a THREE-DIMENSIONAL plot, for put and call options on the same underlying, of days to expiration (x-axis), option strike prices (y-axis), and implied volatilities (z-axis); it SIMULTANEOUSLY shows the volatility skew (smile) and the term structure of implied volatility; typically NOT FLAT as BSM would suggest.

Correct. Per Implied Volatility and Volatility Skew: 'The implied volatility surface can be thought of as a three-dimensional plot, for put and call options on the same underlying asset, of days to expiration (x-axis), option strike prices (y-axis), and implied volatilities (z-axis). It simultaneously shows the volatility skew (smile) and the term structure of implied volatility. Considering that implied volatility varies across different option maturities and displays skew, the implied volatility surface is typically not flat as the BSM model may suggest.'
rationales.C: Incorrect. The surface is built from IMPLIED (not realized) volatility across the strike × expiry grid — three dimensions.

options.A: long calls + short puts; used to implement a BULLISH view (the investor buys the calls) while LOWERING the cost (the investor sells the puts) of the long position in the underlying asset that will be established upon exercising the options.
options.B: A long risk reversal combines a long put with a short call to implement a bearish view.
options.C: A long risk reversal is a delta-neutral volatility position with no directional exposure.

rationales.A:
Full statement: long calls + short puts; used to implement a BULLISH view (the investor buys the calls) while LOWERING the cost (the investor sells the puts) of the long position in the underlying asset that will be established upon exercising the options; combined position aligned with bullish view + declining volatility outlook.

Correct. Per Investment Objectives and Strategy Selection: 'The combined position in which the investor is long calls and short puts (i.e., a long risk reversal) is used to implement a bullish view (the investor buys the calls) while lowering the cost (the investor sells the puts) of the long position in the underlying asset that will be established upon exercising the options.'
rationales.B: Incorrect. Long put plus short call is the SHORT risk reversal — the bearish mirror image.
rationales.C: Incorrect. The structure is directional and BULLISH: long calls financed by short puts, lowering the cost of the upside view.

options.A: Spreads are most appropriate when the investor expects an extreme move far beyond the short strike.
options.B: the purchase of call or put SPREADS is most appropriate when the investor has a bullish view (call bull spread) or a bearish view (put bear spread) but the underlying market is NOT clearly trending upward or downward.
options.C: Outright option purchases are always cheaper than spreads for the same directional view.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Extreme-move conviction favors OUTRIGHT options — the spread's short leg caps the payoff.
rationales.B:
Full statement: the purchase of call or put SPREADS is most appropriate when the investor has a bullish view (call bull spread) or a bearish view (put bear spread) but the underlying market is NOT clearly trending upward or downward; spreads are a way to REDUCE the total cost by buying one option and writing another.

Correct. Per Investment Objectives and Strategy Selection: 'The purchase of call or put spreads tends to be most appropriate when the investor has a bullish view (call bull spread) or a bearish view (put bear spread) but the underlying market is not clearly trending upward or downward. Furthermore, such spreads are a way to reduce the total cost, given that the spread is normally constructed by buying one option and writing another.'
rationales.C: Incorrect. The spread is the CHEAPER structure; the short leg's premium offsets the long leg's cost for a modest directional view.

options.A: A skewed implied volatility curve leaves the cost of a bear put spread unaffected.
options.B: Put skew makes the bear put spread cheaper, since the long put's volatility premium falls.
options.C: if the implied volatility curve is SKEWED — with the implied volatility of OTM puts relatively higher than for nearer-to-the-money options — the cost of a BEARISH SPREAD is EVEN LOWER.

rationales.A: Incorrect. The two legs sit at different points on the skew, so the curve's shape prices directly into the spread.
rationales.B: Incorrect. The effect is the reverse — the deeper-OTM SHORT put carries relatively higher IV, worth less premium offset... the skewed curve makes the spread MORE expensive as the long leg's relative cost rises.
rationales.C:
Full statement: if the implied volatility curve is SKEWED — with the implied volatility of OTM puts relatively higher than for nearer-to-the-money options — the cost of a BEARISH SPREAD is EVEN LOWER; this is because in the put bear spread, the lower exercise price, more OTM (but relatively more expensive) put is SOLD and the higher exercise price, nearer-to-the-money (but relatively less expensive) put is PURCHASED.

Correct. Per Investment Objectives and Strategy Selection: 'Importantly, if the implied volatility curve is skewed, with the implied volatility of OTM puts relatively higher than for nearer-to-the-money options, the cost of a bearish spread is even lower. This is because in the put bear spread, the lower exercise price, more OTM (but relatively more expensive) put is sold and the higher exercise price, nearer-to-the-money (but relatively less expensive) put is purchased.'

options.A: the 170 call generates $32,250 (adequate cash), and given the firm's bearish outlook plus Parker's willingness to sell soon, the risk of shares being called at 170 is acceptable.
options.B: Rivera should recommend the MAY 160 call, since a deep-ITM strike maximizes premium income with no assignment risk.
options.C: Rivera should recommend buying protective puts instead, since Parker's need is income generation.

rationales.A:
Full statement: the 170 call generates $32,250 (adequate cash), and given the firm's bearish outlook plus Parker's willingness to sell soon, the risk of shares being called at 170 is acceptable; the 165 call is in-the-money with high exercise likelihood (undesirable given target is to write, not to be assigned); 175 call would only generate $20,150 (insufficient); 180 call generates $12,500 (also insufficient).

Correct. Per Covered Call Writing (Rivera/Parker): 'If Rivera were to write the MAY 180 calls, doing so would not generate the required cash, $2.50 × 100 × 50 = $12,500. Writing the MAY 165 calls would generate more than enough income: $9.10 × 100 × 50 = $45,500. However, the May 165 call is in the money... Rivera feels there is a high likelihood of Parker's MNZA shares being called away at expiration. Writing the May 175 call would generate only $4.03 × 100 × 50 = $20,150. Although Rivera believes the 175 call would not be exercised, the cash generated would be only about two-thirds of the client's projected need. The May 170 call would generate $6.45 × 100 × 50 = $32,250, which looks good from a cash-generation perspective.'
rationales.B: Incorrect. Deep-ITM writing courts near-certain assignment below the current price; the 170 strike balances the $32,250 cash need against assignment terms Parker can accept.
rationales.C: Incorrect. Puts COST premium — the opposite of raising $30,000 quickly; the covered call generates the cash while fitting the firm's bearish outlook.

options.A: The choice between options and futures/forwards for equity exposure depends only on which instrument has lower commissions.
options.B: Options and futures provide identical exposures, so the choice is arbitrary.
options.C: the choice of derivative depends on the OUTLOOK for the underlying asset, the INVESTMENT HORIZON, and EXPECTATIONS FOR IMPLIED VOLATILITY over that horizon.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Commissions are secondary; the drivers are the OUTLOOK for the underlying, the investment HORIZON, and IMPLIED VOLATILITY expectations.
rationales.B: Incorrect. The payoff shapes differ fundamentally — asymmetric (options) versus symmetric (futures/forwards) — so the view determines the vehicle.
rationales.C:
Full statement: the choice of derivative depends on the OUTLOOK for the underlying asset, the INVESTMENT HORIZON, and EXPECTATIONS FOR IMPLIED VOLATILITY over that horizon; options have asymmetric payoffs while futures have symmetric; futures require margin but no premium; the relative advantage depends on differences in leverage, payoff profiles (asymmetrical vs symmetrical), the shape of the volatility futures term structure, and the cost of the options vs index futures.

Correct. Per Establishing or Modifying Equity Risk Exposure and Solution 3 for Wu: 'The choice of derivative that will satisfy the investment goal depends on the outlook for the underlying asset, the investment horizon, and expectations for implied volatility over that horizon.' And: 'the relative advantage of implementing this long volatility hedging strategy by purchasing calls on the VIX over buying VIX futures depends on the difference in leverage available, the difference in payoff profiles (asymmetrical for options and symmetrical for futures), and the shape of the volatility futures term structure, as well as the cost of the options compared with the cost of the index futures.'

options.A: over-the-counter (OTC) contract between two parties that agree to exchange cash flows on specified payment dates — one based on variable (floating) interest rate and the other based on a fixed rate (the 'swap rate') determined at time swap is initiated.
options.B: An interest rate swap is an exchange-traded contract standardized by the futures clearinghouse.
options.C: An interest rate swap involves the exchange of notional principal amounts at initiation and maturity.

rationales.A:
Full statement: over-the-counter (OTC) contract between two parties that agree to exchange cash flows on specified payment dates — one based on variable (floating) interest rate and the other based on a fixed rate (the 'swap rate') determined at time swap is initiated; swap tenor is when the swap is agreed to expire; both interest rates applied to swap's notional value to determine size of each payment; normally two payments (one fixed, one floating) are in same currency but not equal, so typically netted with party owing greater amount paying difference to other party; party that currently has fixed (floating) risk can effectively convert it into floating (fixed) one.

Correct. Per the reading: 'An interest rate swap is an over-the-counter (OTC) contract between two parties that agree to exchange cash flows on specified payment dates — one based on a variable (floating) interest rate and the other based on a fixed rate (the "swap rate") — determined at the time the swap is initiated. The swap tenor is when the swap is agreed to expire. Both interest rates are applied to the swap's notional value to determine the size of each payment. Normally, the resulting two payments (one fixed, one floating) are in the same currency but will not be equal, so they are typically netted, with the party owing the greater amount paying the difference to the other party. In this manner, a party that currently has a fixed (floating) risk or other obligation can effectively convert it into a floating (fixed) one.'
rationales.B: Incorrect. The IRS is an OTC bilateral contract, not exchange-traded — customization is its hallmark.
rationales.C: Incorrect. In a single-currency IRS the notional is NOT exchanged; only the fixed-versus-floating cash flows change hands.

options.A: hedging results are imperfect because (1) hedge is done with cheapest-to-deliver bond, and since CTD bond can change over holding period, duration of futures contract can also change.
options.B: Interest rate hedges with futures execute perfectly in practice, with no residual basis or duration error.
options.C: The only hedging imperfection is broker commission on the futures trades.

rationales.A:
Full statement: hedging results are imperfect because (1) hedge is done with cheapest-to-deliver bond, and since CTD bond can change over holding period, duration of futures contract can also change; (2) relationship between interest rates and bond prices is not linear, owing to convexity; (3) term structure of interest rates often changes via non-parallel moves.

Correct. Per the reading: 'In the real world, however, the hedging results are imperfect because (1) the hedge is done with the cheapest-to-deliver bond, and since the CTD bond can change over the holding period, the duration of the futures contract can also change; (2) the relationship between interest rates and bond prices is not linear, owing to convexity; and (3) the term structure of interest rates often changes via non-parallel moves.'
rationales.B: Incorrect. Imperfections are structural — the CTD bond can change over the horizon, durations drift, and yield changes are non-parallel.
rationales.C: Incorrect. Commissions are trivial next to CTD switching, duration measurement error, and curve-shape risk.

options.A: The Example 1 firm issues fixed-rate debt and swaps it to floating using a receive-fixed swap.
options.B: firm sells £20 million of three-year floating-rate bonds that pay semiannual coupon equal to six-month reference rate plus 50 bps.
options.C: The Example 1 firm leaves its floating-rate exposure unhedged for the full three years.

rationales.A: Incorrect. The direction is reversed — the firm sells FLOATING-rate bonds and PAYS fixed on the swap to synthesize fixed-rate funding.
rationales.B:
Full statement: firm sells £20 million of three-year floating-rate bonds that pay semiannual coupon equal to six-month reference rate plus 50 bps; firm enters pay-fixed 1.25%, receive-floating six-month reference rate swap; at first coupon date reference rate is 0.75%: coupon on bonds = (0.75% + 0.50%) × £20M × 180/360 = £125,000; on swap: receive 0.75%×£20M×180/360 = £75,000, pay 1.25%×£20M×180/360 = £125,000, net payment £50,000; total = £175,000; at second date rate rises to 1.50%: bond coupon = £200,000, receive £25,000 net on swap, total still £175,000; firm has effectively fixed its all-in borrowing costs.

Correct. Per Example 1: 'An investment firm has sold £20 million of three-year floating-rate bonds that pay a semiannual coupon equal to the six-month market reference rate plus 50 bps... The firm enters into an interest rate swap with a tenor of approximately three years and semiannual payments, where the firm pays a fixed par swap rate of 1.25% and receives the six-month reference rate. At the first swap settlement date, the six-month reference rate is 0.75%.' Solution shows: 'Receive based on the reference rate: 0.75% × £20 million × (180/360) = £75,000. Pay based on the fixed rate: 1.25% × £20 million × (180/360) = £125,000. Net payment made by the firm to swap dealer: £125,000 − £75,000 = £50,000. At the same time, the first semiannual coupon payment on the securities will be (0.75% + 0.50%) × £20 million × (180/360) = £125,000. The total payment made by the investment firm on the securities and the swap is £175,000 (= £125,000 + £50,000)... The investment firm has effectively fixed its all-in borrowing costs.'
rationales.C: Incorrect. The pay-fixed swap converts the £20M floating exposure (reference rate + 50 bps semiannual) into a known all-in fixed cost.

options.A: N_S = [(MDUR_T - MDUR_P) / MDUR_S] × MV_P where MDUR_T is target modified duration, MDUR_P is portfolio modified duration, MDUR_S is swap modified duration, MV_P is market value of bond portfolio.
options.B: The swap notional for a duration target is N_S = (MDUR_T × MDUR_P) / MDUR_S × MV_P.
options.C: The swap notional for a duration target is N_S = MV_P × MDUR_S / (MDUR_T − MDUR_P).

rationales.A:
Full statement: N_S = [(MDUR_T - MDUR_P) / MDUR_S] × MV_P where MDUR_T is target modified duration, MDUR_P is portfolio modified duration, MDUR_S is swap modified duration, MV_P is market value of bond portfolio; equivalence ΔP = N_S × ΔS becomes (MV_P)(MDUR_T - MDUR_P) = (N_S)(MDUR_S).

Correct. Per Equation 1: 'By properly choosing the notional value and the tenor of the swap, the portfolio manager can achieve a combination of the existing portfolio and the interest rate swap that sets the overall portfolio duration to the target duration: (MVP)(MDURP) + (NS)(MDURS) = (MVP)(MDURT). The equivalence ΔP = (NS)(ΔS) becomes (MVP)(MDURT − MDURP) = (NS)(MDURS). To find the swap notional principal, NS, we need to solve for the following formula: NS = [(MDURT − MDURP)/MDURS](MVP).'
rationales.B: Incorrect. The numerator is the duration GAP (MDUR_T − MDUR_P), not the product of durations.
rationales.C: Incorrect. The swap duration DIVIDES the gap; this arrangement inverts the ratio — N_S = [(MDUR_T − MDUR_P)/MDUR_S] × MV_P.

options.A: OTC derivative instrument used mainly to hedge a loan expected to be taken out in the near future or to hedge against changes in the level of interest rates in the future.
options.B: A forward rate agreement is an exchange-traded contract on physical delivery of government bonds.
options.C: A forward rate agreement obligates both parties to exchange the full notional loan amount at settlement.

rationales.A:
Full statement: OTC derivative instrument used mainly to hedge a loan expected to be taken out in the near future or to hedge against changes in the level of interest rates in the future; with advanced settled at maturity, FRA will settle only the discounted difference between the interest rate agreed on in the contract and the actual rate prevailing at time of settlement, applied on the notional amount of the contract.

Correct. Per the reading: 'A forward rate agreement is an OTC derivative instrument that is used mainly to hedge a loan expected to be taken out in the near future or to hedge against changes in the level of interest rates in the future. In fact, with advanced settled at maturity, an FRA will settle only the discounted difference between the interest rate agreed on in the contract and the actual rate prevailing at the time of settlement, applied on the notional amount of the contract.'
rationales.B: Incorrect. The FRA is OTC and cash-settled on a RATE, hedging an expected borrowing or rate exposure.
rationales.C: Incorrect. Only the discounted interest DIFFERENCE settles; the notional never moves.

options.A: Forwards and futures are identical instruments for interest rate hedging in every practical respect.
options.B: forwards, like swaps, are OTC instruments and especially useful because they can be customized, but they have counterparty risk.
options.C: Futures carry counterparty risk while forwards are guaranteed by a central clearinghouse.

rationales.A: Incorrect. The venue difference matters — OTC customization versus exchange standardization drives the choice.
rationales.B:
Full statement: forwards, like swaps, are OTC instruments and especially useful because they can be customized, but they have counterparty risk; in contrast, exchange-traded interest rate futures contracts are standardized and guaranteed by a clearinghouse, so counterparty risk is virtually zero; regulatory changes in global markets are moving both OTC swaps and forward contracts toward a clearing process — reduces risk that one counterparty will default (by requiring collateral for trades and by daily marking to market of open positions); however, still risk that major counterparty will fail to carry out its obligations (e.g., Lehman Brothers).

Correct. Per the reading: 'Forwards, like swaps, are OTC instruments and are especially useful because they can be customized, but they do have counterparty risk. In contrast, exchange-traded interest rate futures contracts are standardized and guaranteed by a clearinghouse, so counterparty risk is virtually zero.' Footnote: 'Regulatory changes in global markets are moving both over-the-counter swaps and forward contracts toward a clearing process as well. The clearing process reduces the risk that one counterparty will default on its obligations (by requiring collateral for trades and by daily marking to market of open positions), and for this reason, the counterparty risk associated with each single trade is virtually zero. However, there is still the risk that a major counterparty will fail to carry out its obligations (e.g., Lehman Brothers), causing operational risks for investors.'
rationales.C: Incorrect. The credit arrangement is the REVERSE: futures are clearinghouse-guaranteed; OTC forwards bear counterparty risk in exchange for customization.

options.A: A currency swap is identical to an interest rate swap in structure and cash flows.
options.B: currency swap is similar to interest rate swap but different in two ways: (1) interest rates are associated with different currencies.
options.C: Currency swaps never involve any exchange of principal amounts.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Two differences separate them — the rates reference DIFFERENT currencies, and notional principals are typically exchanged.
rationales.B:
Full statement: currency swap is similar to interest rate swap but different in two ways: (1) interest rates are associated with different currencies; (2) notional principal amounts may or may not be exchanged at beginning and end of swap's life; currency swaps help parties hedge against risk of exchange rate fluctuations and achieve better rate outcomes; some hedge transactions are designed to hedge only foreign interest cash flows and not principal payments so principal exchange would not be necessary.

Correct. Per the reading: 'A currency swap is similar to an interest rate swap, but it is different in two ways: (1) The interest rates are associated with different currencies, and (2) the notional principal amounts may or may not be exchanged at the beginning and end of the swap's life. Currency swaps help the parties in the swap to hedge against the risk of exchange rate fluctuations and to achieve better rate outcomes.' Footnote: 'Although an exchange of notional principals often occurs, the parties may agree not to do this. Some types of hedge transactions are designed to hedge only foreign interest cash flows and not principal payments, so a principal exchange on a currency swap would not be necessary.'
rationales.C: Incorrect. Principal exchange (at initiation and maturity) is one of the currency swap's two distinguishing features.

options.A: The cross-currency basis is fully explained by covered interest rate parity holding at all times.
options.B: if covered interest rate parity held perfectly, forward exchange rate would be determined solely by spot exchange rate and interest rate differential between currencies.
options.C: Forward exchange rates are set independently of spot rates and interest differentials.

rationales.A: Incorrect. The basis exists precisely because CIRP does NOT hold perfectly — funding frictions leave a residual.
rationales.B:
Full statement: if covered interest rate parity held perfectly, forward exchange rate would be determined solely by spot exchange rate and interest rate differential between currencies; deviations from covered interest rate parity give rise to the basis in cross-currency basis swaps; the basis represents difference between interest rates in the cross-currency basis swap and those implied by forward exchange rates; typically not zero.

Correct. Per the reading: 'Here the basis is the difference between interest rates in the cross-currency basis swap and those used to determine the forward exchange rates. If covered interest rate parity holds, a forward exchange rate is determined by the spot exchange rate and the interest rate differential between foreign and domestic currencies over the term of the forward rate. However, usually covered interest rate parity does not hold and thus gives rise to the basis.'
rationales.C: Incorrect. Spot and rate differentials remain the forward's foundation; the basis is the deviation layered on top.

options.A: A cross-currency basis swap avoids exchanging notional principals to minimize settlement risk.
options.B: A cross-currency basis swap exchanges fixed rates in one currency for fixed rates in the same currency.
options.C: exchanges notional principals because goal of transaction is to issue at more favorable funding rate and swap the amount back to currency of choice.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Notionals ARE exchanged — the transaction's purpose is funding in one currency swapped into another.
rationales.B: Incorrect. Same-currency fixed-for-fixed is not a cross-currency structure; the basis swap moves favorable funding across currencies.
rationales.C:
Full statement: exchanges notional principals because goal of transaction is to issue at more favorable funding rate and swap the amount back to currency of choice; firms that need foreign-denominated cash can obtain funding in local currency and then swap local currency for required foreign currency using cross-currency basis swap; swap periodically sets interest rate payments, mostly floating for floating, separately in two different currencies; net effect is to use loan in local currency to take out loan in foreign currency while avoiding foreign exchange risk; exchange rate is fixed.

Correct. Per the reading: 'In particular, a cross-currency basis swap exchanges notional principals because the goal of the transaction is to issue at a more favorable funding rate and swap the amount back to the currency of choice. Firms that need foreign-denominated cash can obtain the funding in their local currency and then swap the local currency for the required foreign currency using a cross-currency basis swap. The swap periodically sets interest rate payments, mostly floating for floating, separately in two different currencies. The net effect is to use a loan in a local currency to take out a loan in a foreign currency while avoiding any foreign exchange risk. In fact, the exchange rate is fixed.'

options.A: on first settlement date CAD reference rate 1.95%, basis -15 bps → CAD rate on swap is 1.80% (= 1.95% - 15 bps).
options.B: On the first settlement date, the CAD rate on the swap equals the 1.95% reference rate plus the 15 bp basis, or 2.10%.
options.C: The basis adjustment applies to the USD leg of the swap rather than the CAD leg.

rationales.A:
Full statement: on first settlement date CAD reference rate 1.95%, basis -15 bps → CAD rate on swap is 1.80% (= 1.95% - 15 bps); assume USD rate 2.50%; PE firm receives CAD50M × 1.80% × 180/360 = CAD450,000; PE firm pays CAD50M × 0.8000 USD/CAD × 2.50% × 180/360 = USD500,000; interest on PE firm's loan is CAD50M × (1.95% + 0.65%) × 180/360 = CAD650,000; net Canadian dollar payment = CAD200,000 (= CAD650,000 - CAD450,000); at USD/CAD 0.8000 this equals USD160,000, plus USD500,000 on swap = USD660,000 total vs USD700,000 (= USD40M × 3.5% × 180/360) if firm had taken USD loan.

Correct. Per Example 7: 'the Canadian dollar rate on the swap is 1.80% (= 1.95% − 15 bps), and we assume the US dollar rate is 2.50%... CAD50 million × 1.80% × 180/360 = CAD450,000 (A). PE Firm → Swap Dealer. CAD50million × 0.8000 USD/CAD × 2.50% × 180/360 = USD500,000 (B). The interest rate payment on the PE firm's loan is CAD50 million × (1.95% + 0.65%) × (180/360) = CAD650,000. Considering the CAD450,000 received on the swap (A), the PE firm's net payment is CAD200,000. At USD/CAD 0.8000, this net payment of CAD200,000 corresponds to a payment of USD160,000, which when added to the USD500,000 paid on the swap (B) totals USD660,000. Importantly, note that had the Canadian PE firm taken out the US loan instead, it would have paid periodically USD700,000 (= USD40 million × [2.50% + 1.0%] × [180/360]).'
rationales.B: Incorrect. The basis is NEGATIVE — it subtracts: 1.95% − 15 bps = 1.80%.
rationales.C: Incorrect. The −15 bp basis attaches to the CAD leg in this structure, lowering the CAD rate to 1.80%.

options.A: At maturity of the cross-currency swap there are no cash flows, since all principal was settled at initiation.
options.B: at maturity of swap (after successful exit from LBO via US IPO), Canadian PE firm swaps back US dollars in exchange for Canadian dollars at same rate: PE Firm returns USD40 million to swap dealer, receives CAD50 million (= USD40M × 1/0.8000).
options.C: At maturity the Canadian PE firm keeps the US dollars and pays only accrued interest.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Maturity brings the REVERSE principal exchange — that is how the firm unwinds the funding.
rationales.B:
Full statement: at maturity of swap (after successful exit from LBO via US IPO), Canadian PE firm swaps back US dollars in exchange for Canadian dollars at same rate: PE Firm returns USD40 million to swap dealer, receives CAD50 million (= USD40M × 1/0.8000); PE firm uses CAD50M to pay off local lender.

Correct. Per Example 7: 'Flows at maturity. At the maturity of the swap (and after a successful exit from the LBO via a US IPO), the Canadian PE firm swaps back US dollars in exchange for Canadian dollars (USD × 1/S0). CAD50 million (= USD40million × 1/0.8000) PE Firm → Swap Dealer, USD40 million.' Also from setup: 'the PE firm returns the USD40 million to the dealer and in return receives the CAD50 million, which it uses to pay off its lender.'
rationales.C: Incorrect. Following the IPO exit, the firm swaps the US dollars BACK for Canadian dollars at the original S₀.

options.A: Currency risk is the risk that domestic interest rates rise, lowering domestic bond prices.
options.B: Currency risk applies only to assets, never to liabilities denominated in foreign currencies.
options.C: risk that value of a current or future asset (liability) in a foreign currency will decrease (increase) when converted into the domestic currency.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Domestic rate risk is interest rate risk; currency risk concerns CONVERSION values across currencies.
rationales.B: Incorrect. Liabilities carry it symmetrically — a foreign-currency liability grows in domestic terms when the foreign currency appreciates.
rationales.C:
Full statement: risk that value of a current or future asset (liability) in a foreign currency will decrease (increase) when converted into the domestic currency; currency swaps, forwards, and futures can be used to effectively alter currency risk exposures.

Correct. Per the reading: 'Currency swaps, forwards, and futures can be used to effectively alter currency risk exposures. Currency risk is the risk that the value of a current or future asset (liability) in a foreign currency will decrease (increase) when converted into the domestic currency.'

options.A: N is notional principal of Canadian dollar leg of swap, and S_0 (agreed at start) is spot exchange rate for all payments (at inception, on interest payment dates, and at maturity).
options.B: The exchange rate for each payment in a cross-currency swap resets to the prevailing spot rate at each settlement date.
options.C: The notional principal of the swap is redenominated at every payment date using updated market rates.

rationales.A:
Full statement: N is notional principal of Canadian dollar leg of swap, and S_0 (agreed at start) is spot exchange rate for all payments (at inception, on interest payment dates, and at maturity); for Canadian PE firm this means N = CAD50 million and S_0 = USD/CAD 0.8000; the exchange rate is fixed for all cash flows over life of swap, avoiding FX risk on principal exchange.

Correct. Per Example 7: 'We now examine the cash flows in the cross-currency basis swap, where N is the notional principal of the Canadian dollar leg of the swap and S0, agreed at the start, is the spot exchange rate for all payments (at inception, on interest payment dates, and at maturity). For the Canadian PE firm, this means that N = CAD50 million and S0 = USD/CAD 0.8000.'
rationales.B: Incorrect. S₀ is FIXED at inception and governs all payments — initial exchange, periodic interest, and final re-exchange.
rationales.C: Incorrect. The notional and its conversion rate are locked at the start; no redenomination occurs.

options.A: A total return swap on a concentrated stock position increases the holder's exposure to the stock's price movements.
options.B: investor holding large concentrated position paying dividends can enter total return swap requiring her to transfer total performance of stock to counterparty for prespecified fee.
options.C: A total return swap requires the investor to sell the concentrated position, triggering immediate capital gains tax.

rationales.A: Incorrect. The TRS NEUTRALIZES the exposure — total performance (dividends and price change) is paid away for a floating receipt.
rationales.B:
Full statement: investor holding large concentrated position paying dividends can enter total return swap requiring her to transfer total performance of stock to counterparty for prespecified fee; under swap she pays counterparty share price appreciation plus dividends received over life of contract; if stock price decreases, she receives share price depreciation but net of dividends; investor receives agreed-on floating-rate interest payment based on swap notional; allows temporary neutralization of long exposure without losing ownership or voting rights.

Correct. Per the reading: 'Consider now a private high-net-worth investor who holds a large, concentrated position in a particular company's stock that pays dividends on a regular basis. She expects that in the next six months the total return from the stock, including the dividends received, will be negative, so she wants to temporarily neutralize her long exposure. At the same time, she does not wish to lose ownership and her voting rights by selling the stock on an exchange. This investor can enter into a total return swap requiring her to transfer the total performance of the stock (i.e., total return) to the counterparty of the swap, at prespecified dates for an agreed-on fee. Under the terms of the swap, she will pay to the counterparty the share price appreciation plus the dividends received over the life of the contract. If the stock price decreases, she will receive the share price depreciation but net of the dividends. At the same prespecified dates, the investor will receive in exchange from the counterparty an agreed-on floating-rate interest payment based on the swap notional.'
rationales.C: Incorrect. No sale occurs; deferring the tax event while shedding economic exposure is the structure's appeal.

options.A: A total return swap leaves the equity risk of the swapped portion in place while adding funding cost.
options.B: total return swap effectively removes risk associated with portion of equity portfolio allocation and converts it into money market equivalent returns.
options.C: The TRS converts the equity allocation into additional equity exposure at higher beta.

rationales.A: Incorrect. The swap REMOVES the equity risk of the covered portion — that is the transfer.
rationales.B:
Full statement: total return swap effectively removes risk associated with portion of equity portfolio allocation and converts it into money market equivalent returns; test of reasonableness: portfolio comprising 70% equities and 30% money market instruments assumed to earn 2% (1% over six months) would achieve return of 3.8% (= 0.7 × 5% + 0.3 × [2%/2]) or $3.8M on $100M in bullish scenario; in bearish scenario return would be -3.2% (= 0.7 × -5% + 0.3 × [2%/2]) or -$3.2M.

Correct. Per the reading: 'To test the reasonableness of the result, a portfolio comprising 70% equities and 30% money market instruments assumed to earn 2% (1% over the six months) would achieve a return of 3.8% (= 0.7 × 5% + 0.3 × [2%/2]) or $3.8 million on $100 million in the bullish scenario (Scenario 1). In the bearish scenario (Scenario 2), the return would be −3.2% (= 0.7 × −5% + 0.3 × [2%/2]) or −$3.2 million on the initial $100 million portfolio. The total return swap effectively removes the risk associated with 30% of the equity portfolio allocation and converts it into money market equivalent returns.'
rationales.C: Incorrect. The conversion runs equity → money-market-like return, not equity → leveraged equity.

options.A: portfolio manager is receiver in six-month equity swap with notional principal €4.5 million and no interim cash flows requiring physical settlement at maturity of 300,000 shares of Italian insurer Generali.
options.B: In the Generali example, physical and cash settlement produce materially different economic outcomes for the manager.
options.C: The Generali equity swap requires daily margin postings and interim cash flows throughout its six-month term.

rationales.A:
Full statement: portfolio manager is receiver in six-month equity swap with notional principal €4.5 million and no interim cash flows requiring physical settlement at maturity of 300,000 shares of Italian insurer Generali; at maturity manager receives 300,000 shares and pays €4.5 million (purchase price per share €15 = €4.5M/300,000); also pays interest on swap; at swap maturity Generali price is €16 implying gain of €300,000 (= [€16 - €15] × 300,000); if cash settled instead of physical, manager would have received €300,000 = (€16 - €15) × 300,000.

Correct. Per the reading: 'Let us assume for example that a portfolio manager is the receiver in a six-month equity swap with notional principal of €4.5 million and no interim cash flows that requires physical settlement, at maturity, of 300,000 shares of the Italian insurer Generali. At maturity of the swap, the portfolio manager will receive 300,000 shares of Generali and will pay €4.5 million, which corresponds to a purchase price per share of €15 (= €4.5 million/300,000). He will also pay the interest on the swap based on the agreed-on rate. Now let us also assume that at the swap's maturity the price of Generali is €16. This price implies a gain of €300,000 (= [€16 − €15] × 300,000) for the portfolio manager, assuming he sells the shares received in the swap at €16. If the same swap had cash settlement, instead of physical settlement, at maturity the portfolio manager would have received €300,000 — given by €16 (the swap settlement price) less €15 (the agreed price on the swap) and multiplied by 300,000 — against the payment of the interest on the swap.'
rationales.B: Incorrect. The €300K economic outcome is the SAME either way — settlement mechanics differ, economics do not.
rationales.C: Incorrect. The structure has NO interim cash flows; the receiver's position settles at the six-month maturity on the €4.5M notional.

options.A: Equity index futures are used only by speculators and have no role in portfolio management.
options.B: indispensable tool for many investment managers: low-cost instrument to implement tactical allocation decisions, achieve portfolio diversification, and attain international exposure.
options.C: Equity index futures are too expensive for tactical asset allocation relative to trading the underlying stocks.

rationales.A: Incorrect. They are an indispensable MANAGER tool — tactical shifts, beta adjustment, cash equitization, rebalancing.
rationales.B:
Full statement: indispensable tool for many investment managers: low-cost instrument to implement tactical allocation decisions, achieve portfolio diversification, and attain international exposure; standardized contracts listed on exchange; when underlying is stock index, only cash settlement is available at contract expiration; single stock futures also available to acquire desired exposure to specific stock (this exposure also achievable with equity forwards which are OTC contracts used when counterparties need customized agreement); at expiration, single stock futures contract could require cash settlement or physical settlement using stock.

Correct. Per the reading: 'Equity index futures are an indispensable tool for many investment managers: They are a low-cost instrument to implement tactical allocation decisions, achieve portfolio diversification, and attain international exposure. They are standardized contracts listed on an exchange, and when the underlying is a stock index, only cash settlement is available at contract expiration. Single stock futures are also available to investors to acquire the desired exposure to a specific stock. This exposure is also achievable with equity forwards, which are OTC contracts that are used when the counterparties need a customized agreement. The underlying of a single stock futures contract is one specified stock, and the investor can receive or pay its performance. At expiration, the contract could require cash settlement or physical settlement using the stock.'
rationales.C: Incorrect. Cost runs the other way: futures are typically the CHEAPEST route to index exposure changes.

options.A: The futures hedge ratio for beta targeting is N_f = (β_T + β_S) / β_f × (F/S).
options.B: The futures hedge ratio for beta targeting is N_f = β_T × β_S × β_f × (S/F).
options.C: N_f = [(β_T - β_S) / β_f] × (S/F) where β_T is target beta, β_S is portfolio beta, β_f is beta of futures, S is market value of stock portfolio, F is value per futures contract.

rationales.A: Incorrect. The numerator is the beta GAP (β_T − β_S), and the value ratio is S/F, not F/S.
rationales.B: Incorrect. Multiplying the betas has no meaning — N_f = [(β_T − β_S)/β_f] × (S/F).
rationales.C:
Full statement: N_f = [(β_T - β_S) / β_f] × (S/F) where β_T is target beta, β_S is portfolio beta, β_f is beta of futures, S is market value of stock portfolio, F is value per futures contract; if investor wants to increase portfolio's beta, β_T > β_S and N_f is positive (must buy futures); if she wants to decrease beta, β_T < β_S and N_f is negative (must sell futures); special case eliminating market risk β_T = 0 reduces to N_f = -(β_S/β_f)(S/F).

Correct. Per Equation 10: 'N_f = [(βT − βS)/βf] × (S/F). Note that if the investor wants to increase the portfolio's beta, βT will exceed βS and the sign of Nf will be positive, which means that she must buy futures. If she wants to decrease the beta, βT will be less than βS, the sign of Nf will be negative, and she must sell futures. This relationship should make sense: Selling futures will offset some of the risk of holding the stock, whereas buying futures will add risk. In the special case in which the goal is to eliminate market risk, βT would be set to zero and the formula would reduce to Nf = −(βS/βf)(S/F). In this case, the sign of Nf will always be negative, which makes sense.'

options.A: Long volatility exposure amplifies losses in an equity sell-off because volatility falls when prices fall.
options.B: long volatility exposure can be effective hedge against sell-off in long equity portfolio, notably during periods of extreme market movements.
options.C: Volatility exposure bears no relationship to equity portfolio performance.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Volatility RISES in sell-offs — the negative price-volatility correlation is what makes long vol a hedge.
rationales.B:
Full statement: long volatility exposure can be effective hedge against sell-off in long equity portfolio, notably during periods of extreme market movements; empirical studies have identified a negative correlation between volatility and stock index returns that becomes pronounced during stock market downturns; variance swaps have valuable convexity feature — as realized volatility increases (decreases), positive (negative) swap payoffs increase (decrease) — that makes them particularly attractive for hedging long equity portfolios.

Correct. Per the reading: 'With the introduction of volatility futures and variance swaps, many investors now consider volatility an asset class in itself. In particular, long volatility exposure can be an effective hedge against a sell-off in a long equity portfolio, notably during periods of extreme market movements. Empirical studies have identified a negative correlation between volatility and stock index returns that becomes pronounced during stock market downturns. Importantly, variance swaps... have a valuable convexity feature — as realized volatility increases (decreases), the positive (negative) swap payoffs increase (decrease) — that makes them particularly attractive for hedging long equity portfolios.'
rationales.C: Incorrect. The relationship is strong and negative, and most powerful in extreme downturns — precisely when the hedge is needed.

options.A: Santos receives $95,000 at settlement because realized volatility of 18% fell below the 20% strike.
options.B: The settlement equals the vega notional times the volatility difference: 1,250 × (18 − 20) = −$2,500.
options.C: SettlementT = Variance notional × (Realized variance - Variance strike) = 1,250 × (18² - 20²) = 1,250 × (324 - 400) = -$95,000.

rationales.A: Incorrect. As the LONG variance party with realized below strike, Santos PAYS — the settlement is negative to him.
rationales.B: Incorrect. Settlement runs on VARIANCE units: 1,250 × (18² − 20²) = 1,250 × (−76) = −$95,000.
rationales.C:
Full statement: SettlementT = Variance notional × (Realized variance - Variance strike) = 1,250 × (18² - 20²) = 1,250 × (324 - 400) = -$95,000; since settlement is negative, swap buyer pays swap seller; Santos (seller) receives $95,000 from swap buyer.

Correct. Per Example 12: 'The settlement amount is calculated using Equation 12 as follows: SettlementT = Variance notional × (Realized variance − Variance strike) = 1,250 × (18² − 20²) = −$95,000. If the payment amount is positive (negative), the swap seller (buyer) pays the swap buyer (seller). So, in this case, Santos would receive $95,000 from the swap buyer.'

options.A: The VIX index measures realized volatility of the S&P 500 over the past 30 days.
options.B: The VIX index measures expected dividend yields on the S&P 500 over the next year.
options.C: CBOE Volatility Index or 'fear index' is a measure of investors' expectations of volatility in S&P 500 over next 30 days.

rationales.A: Incorrect. The VIX is FORWARD-looking — implied, not realized, volatility.
rationales.B: Incorrect. Dividends are not its subject; it gauges expected S&P 500 volatility over the NEXT 30 days — the 'fear index'.
rationales.C:
